skill.name: distributed-tracing
skill.description: Guides teams through distributed tracing setup with Jaeger, Tempo, OpenTelemetry instrumentation, context propagation, sampling, and trace analysis.
skill.summary: Implement distributed tracing across microservices with Jaeger, Tempo, and OpenTelemetry.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, dynamic import, weak crypto, and reconnaissance patterns. Review shows most are false positives from Markdown fences, language examples, tracing endpoints, and trace ID formatting, with no prompt injection or malicious exfiltration found. Medium publication warnings remain because the guide includes copyable Kubernetes commands that fetch remote manifests and examples that use mutable latest container tags.

medium_findings.title: Remote Kubernetes Manifest Deployment Example
medium_findings.description: The Jaeger setup includes a copyable kubectl command that creates a namespace and installs an operator manifest from a GitHub URL. This is legitimate observability guidance, but applying remote manifests can modify a cluster and should be reviewed before use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is plainly shown in a Kubernetes deployment example and fetches an external manifest. It is not malicious by itself, but it can change cluster state if copied and run.

medium_findings.title: Mutable Container Image Tags in Deployment Examples
medium_findings.description: The Docker Compose and Tempo deployment examples use latest image tags. Mutable tags create supply chain and reproducibility risk because the deployed image can change over time without a content review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly use latest tags for Jaeger and Tempo images. This is a known deployment hygiene risk, although it does not show malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Markdown Fence Patterns Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer treated many Markdown backticks and fenced examples as Ruby or shell backtick execution. These are documentation examples, not executable code embedded in the skill runtime.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are visible Markdown references or fenced code examples. I did not find Ruby backtick execution or a hidden command runner in the skill file.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Import Finding Is a Go Import Block
low_findings.description: The dynamic import alert points to a Go code example import block for OpenTelemetry packages. It does not load attacker-controlled modules or execute dynamic code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are a static Go import section inside a fenced example. No JavaScript dynamic import expression is present at that location.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Is Frontmatter Text
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alert points to the description line, which says distributed tracing. No cryptographic algorithm or cryptographic operation is present there.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line contains only metadata text about distributed tracing. I did not find DES, MD5, SHA1, or another weak cryptographic use at that location.

low_findings.title: Network Examples Are Observability Endpoints
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs and HTTP client calls are examples for Jaeger, Elasticsearch, Tempo, and downstream-service trace propagation. They do not send secrets to an unrelated external endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are tied to tracing collectors, internal service names, or a pinned Jaeger operator release. I did not find credential collection or exfiltration semantics.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Finding Is Trace ID Formatting
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance alert points to reading the current OpenTelemetry span context and formatting its trace ID for correlated logging. This is normal tracing behavior, not host or network reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited code accesses span context within an observability example. It does not enumerate files, users, processes, interfaces, or other system state.

dangerous_patterns.title: Copyable Cluster Mutation Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide includes kubectl commands that create Kubernetes resources. Users should review manifests and run them only against intended clusters.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are legitimate setup instructions but can alter cluster state. The risk depends on user execution context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Mutable Image Reference
dangerous_patterns.description: Deployment examples use latest tags instead of immutable versions or digests. This can change deployed code without a corresponding skill update.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The mutable image tags are directly visible in deployment examples. This is a real operational risk, not a malware indicator.

content.value_statement: Microservice failures are hard to debug when requests cross many services. This skill guides tracing setup, instrumentation, sampling, and analysis with Jaeger, Tempo, and OpenTelemetry.
actual_capabilities: Explains trace, span, context, tag, and log concepts for distributed systems.
Provides Jaeger setup examples for Kubernetes and Docker Compose environments.
Shows OpenTelemetry instrumentation patterns for Python Flask, Node.js Express, and Go services.
Demonstrates trace context propagation through HTTP headers and client requests.
Provides Tempo configuration examples, sampling strategies, trace queries, and troubleshooting checks.
limitations: It is a guide and does not install or validate tracing infrastructure automatically.
Referenced resource files are not present in this package, so only SKILL.md content is available.
Examples need adaptation for production security, storage, networking, and version pinning.
It does not cover every tracing backend, language SDK, or managed observability platform.

use_cases.title: Debug Microservice Latency
use_cases.description: Trace slow requests across gateways, services, and databases to locate the operations that add the most latency.

use_cases.title: Create an Observability Baseline
use_cases.description: Plan Jaeger or Tempo deployment, choose sampling settings, and define consistent instrumentation patterns for service teams.

use_cases.title: Investigate Error Propagation
use_cases.description: Use trace queries, correlated logs, and service dependency views to understand where failures begin and spread.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Tracing Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the distributed-tracing skill to plan a basic Jaeger setup for my microservices. Ask for missing environment details first.

prompt_templates.title: Instrument a Service
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the distributed-tracing skill to add OpenTelemetry tracing to a Python Flask service. Include spans, attributes, and exporter setup.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze a Slow Request
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the distributed-tracing skill to review this request flow and identify likely bottlenecks, missing spans, and useful Jaeger queries.

prompt_templates.title: Design Production Tracing Standards
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the distributed-tracing skill to define production tracing standards for sampling, context propagation, span naming, tags, logging correlation, and rollout verification.

output_examples.input: I need tracing for a Flask service that calls a database and another internal API.
output_examples.output: A concise implementation plan covering tracer provider setup, Flask auto-instrumentation, custom spans for database and API calls, context propagation, and collector endpoint checks.

output_examples.input: Our checkout requests are slow and traces show several downstream services.
output_examples.output: A trace analysis checklist that compares span duration, error tags, service dependencies, sampling settings, and correlated logs to isolate the likely bottleneck.

output_examples.input: We want Tempo in Kubernetes with S3 storage.
output_examples.output: A deployment review that identifies required Tempo receivers, storage settings, network access, version pinning, resource sizing, and validation steps.

best_practices: Pin container images and review remote manifests before running deployment commands.
Use consistent service names, span names, and semantic attributes across all services.
Start with conservative sampling and verify tracing overhead before broad production rollout.
anti_patterns: Do not store sensitive user data, tokens, or full request bodies in span attributes.
Do not copy Kubernetes commands into production without reviewing namespaces, RBAC, storage, and network exposure.
Do not instrument only entry services and ignore downstream calls, background jobs, and database operations.

faq.question: Does this skill install Jaeger or Tempo automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides setup and instrumentation guidance that a user must review and apply in their own environment.

faq.question: Which tracing tools does it focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on Jaeger, Grafana Tempo, and OpenTelemetry instrumentation patterns.

faq.question: Can it help with existing trace data?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide trace query design, bottleneck analysis, error investigation, and logging correlation.

faq.question: Is it safe for production use?
faq.answer: It can support production planning, but deployment examples need security review, pinned versions, and environment-specific changes.

faq.question: Does it cover multiple programming languages?
faq.answer: Yes. The included examples cover Python Flask, Node.js Express, and Go instrumentation.

faq.question: What should users provide before asking for help?
faq.answer: Provide service names, runtime languages, deployment platform, tracing backend, current symptoms, and any existing trace or log evidence.
